Why Do Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia Lesions Regress? Unveiling the Single-Cell Spatial Biomarkers

Naipunya Guruprasad1,4, Ella Anttila1, Zhihan Liang1, Kaiyang Zhang1,3, Saundarya Shah1,Oskari Lehtonen1, Laura Bergqvist2, Karoliina Aro2, Sampsa Hautaniemi1, Ilkka Kalliala2,5, Anniina Färkkilä*1,2,4, Anni Virtanen*1,3

1) Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Faculty of Medicine, Finland 2) Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland 3) Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki, University Hospital, Finland 4) Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland 5) Department of Metabolism, Digestion and Reproduction, Faculty of Medicine, Institute of Reproductive and Developmental Biology, Imperial College, London, UK. *correspondence

Background

Cervical cancer ranks as the fourth most prevalent cancer among women globally, causing 349,000 fatalities in 2022.

Aims

The mechanism by which HPV-associated neoplasias either regress or progress remains unknown, but it is influenced by the tumour microenvironment (TME), its spatial configuration, and cellular interactions.

Conclusions

Material and Methods

We performed Tissue Cyclic Immunofluorescence (t-CyCIF) on 65 CIN2 samples and segmented 〜 3M cells.

Cell phenotyping revealed distinct phenotypes across epithelial, stromal, and immune populations.

From GeoMx, we’ve collected 243 regions of interest from 17 samples, capturing around 6,000 transcripts per region that will allow us to us to connect gene expression with cellular and spatial Information.

Patients and their family

Results

Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia (CIN2) represents an intermediate stage of precancerous change in the cervical epithelium where lesions can either regress or progress to higher-grade diseases. (CIN3 or invasive cancer)

Next Steps

Bitsouni et al, From primary HPV infection to carcinoma in situ: A mathematical approach to cervical intraepithelial neoplasia. Studies in Applied Mathematics, 152(1), Article 12697.

Cohort description

2. t-CyCIF Antibody panel

We profiled our cohort of CIN2 lesions using this 22-plex t-CyCIF panel, which included a mix of lineage and functional markers to identify the cell types along with their cell states within the microenvironment.

2. Performing spatial analysis

To define more specific Lymphoid and Myeloid cell types

Applying Recurrent Cell Neighborhood (RCN) analysis to explore the spatial organization within the lesion.
